page: Google Cloud Platform c3d-highcpu-90 is a compute-optimized server instance powered by an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. It features 90 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 45 physical cores with multithreading enabled. The system is equipped with 177.0 GB of system memory, yielding approximately 1.97 GB of RAM per core. There is no bundled local storage or GPU hardware included with this instance type.

In benchmark evaluations, the instance demonstrates top-tier multi-core CPU performance, scoring 129122.14 in stress-ng multi-core tests and 87356.8 in PassMark CPU evaluations. It also achieves top-tier results in software compilation with Clang and ray tracing. Cryptographic operations are highly efficient, supported by hardware-accelerated instruction sets like AES, VAES, and SHA_NI, which is reflected in a top-tier PassMark CPU encryption score. Memory read bandwidth is strong, reaching 215.31 GB/sec for large uncached blocks, though write bandwidth for large blocks remains average at 78.98 GB/sec.

Given the high compute density and the lack of local storage, this instance is structured for compute-heavy workloads that do not require massive memory footprints or local disk caching. It is highly effective for software compilation, ray tracing, cryptographic processing, and medium-scale language model inference. The qualitative cost profile is optimized for raw CPU throughput per resource unit, though workloads requiring high memory capacity relative to compute or local NVMe storage will face trade-offs.
description: Google Cloud Platform c3d-highcpu-90 is a compute-optimized server featuring 90 dedicated vCPUs and 177.0 GB of RAM, powered by an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor on the x86_64 architecture. With 45 physical cores and multithreading, it delivers top-tier multi-core CPU performance, as evidenced by strong stress-ng and PassMark CPU scores. The instance lacks local storage and GPU hardware, focusing resources entirely on compute density. It excels in software compilation, ray tracing, and cryptographic operations, supported by hardware-accelerated instruction sets like AVX-512 and VAES. Memory read bandwidth is strong, though write bandwidth is average. This configuration is highly efficient for compute-bound workloads such as parallel processing, medium-scale LLM inference, and static web serving, offering a high ratio of processing power to memory.
